content.thinking:
Now I understand the problem. The producer uses credentials from `rabbitmq-app` secret. It tries to declare a queue in the `/app` vhost with:
```
payload='{"durable":true,"auto_delete":false,"arguments":{"x-queue-type":"classic","x-queue-mode":"lazy"}}'
```

The permissions show that `app-user` has `write` and `read` but the `configure` field is EMPTY - so `app-user` cannot configure (declare/delete) queues. The `admin` user has `.*` for all.

Let me check what username is in the `rabbitmq-app` secret:

content.text: The issue is visible: `app-user` has no `configure` permission on the `/app` vhost (the configure column is empty), so it can't declare the queue. Let me verify the secret username and fix the permissions.

input.command: kubectl -n karma-rabbitmq-classic-failover-20260-1371eb16-cluster-a get secret rabbitmq-app -o jsonpath='{.data.username}' | base64 -d 2>&1
input.description: Decode the username from rabbitmq-app secret

content.thinking:
The secret username is `app-user`. The issue is that `app-user` has no configure permission on `/app` vhost (configure is empty). It needs `.*` configure permission to be able to declare queues.

I need to set the permissions for `app-user` on the `/app` vhost to include configure permission:

```
rabbitmqctl set_permissions -p /app app-user ".*" ".*" ".*"
```

This will grant `app-user` full configure, write, and read permissions on the `/app` vhost.

content.text: Confirmed: `app-user` has no configure permission on `/app`, which blocks queue declaration. I'll grant full permissions now.

input.command: kubectl -n karma-rabbitmq-classic-failover-20260-1371eb16-cluster-a exec rabbitmq-0 -- rabbitmqctl set_permissions -p /app app-user ".*" ".*" ".*" 2>&1
input.description: Grant app-user full permissions on /app vhost

description: Grant app-user full permissions on /app vhost

summary: Grant app-user full permissions on /app vhost
